title: Red Wine on Cotton
stainChemistry: Red wine contains anthocyanin polyphenols (tannins)—the pigments responsible for wine color. White cotton is pure cellulose, extremely tolerant of aggressive chemistry: enzymes, bleach, heat, strong alkali. Anthocyanins bond to cellulose through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. The tannin color is resistant to water alone but vulnerable to oxidation and alkalinity. Fresh red wine can be removed with boiling water pour-through; older stains require oxidizer or bleach.
whyThisWorks: Cold NSD (NSD) removes surface tannin while suspending pigment. Tannin spotter (acetic acid or specialized tannin remover) breaks hydrogen bonds in anthocyanin structure. H₂O₂ oxidizes remaining color into colorless compounds. White cotton's tolerance for heat and bleach makes red wine one of the easiest stains to eliminate completely.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Immediately blot with cold water from the back. Press hard to absorb wine. Most fresh wine transfers to cloth in first 60 seconds. Flip cloth frequently and continue blotting. Cold water prevents tannin setting.

Mix cold water with 1 teaspoon NSD . Sponge onto stain. NSD suspends anthocyanin pigment without setting it. Work gently with sponge. After 2-3 minutes, blot with white cloth. Stain should lighten noticeably.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Kettle of boiling water, towel underneath
spottingProtocol.instruction: For very fresh stains (within 30 minutes), hot water pour-through can be remarkably effective. Stretch fabric over a bowl or sink with towel underneath. Pour hot (not boiling) water through from the back. Heat helps break anthocyanin bonds on fresh stains. Do NOT use this for aged stains — heat can set older tannin permanently.

If wine color remains (orange-brown), apply diluted 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) (1 part vinegar to 1 part cold water). Acetic acid further disrupts hydrogen bonds in anthocyanin. Apply with dropper. After 2-3 minutes, blot. Continue blotting until no more color transfers.

Flush thoroughly with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully removed before drying.

After spotting, wash in hot water with regular laundry detergent. Hot water removes residual tannin and any treatment chemicals. White cotton can tolerate boiling (95°C). Once washed and dried, any remaining faint color often fades with sunlight exposure.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test any solution on an inside seam first. If color transfers to the cloth, stop and take the garment to a dry cleaner immediately.
If the spill is fresh, blot firmly from the back with a clean white cloth and cold water. Work edge-to-center. Never rub. Most fresh red wine lifts in the first 60 seconds.

If the stain is under 30 minutes old, stretch the fabric over a bowl, place a towel underneath, and pour hot (not boiling) water through from the back. Skip this step if older — heat will set the tannin permanently.
Mix 1 tsp Dawn dish soap, 2 tbsp white vinegar, and 1 cup cold water. Sponge onto the stain, let sit 3 minutes, then blot center outward. If color remains, repeat once.
Flush with cold water from the reverse until your cloth runs clean. No soap or vinegar residue should remain.
Mix 1 scoop OxiClean in 1 cup cold water. Soak the stained area 15–30 minutes. Rinse cold.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or hydrogen peroxide — one oxidizer at a time.

homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect the stain before drying — heat sets any remaining tannin.
If any trace remains after washing, take the garment to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Red wine on white cotton, pre-treated at home with dish soap, vinegar, and OxiClean."

materialWarnings: Boiling water pour-through only for FRESH stains (within 30 min)
For aged wine stains, skip boiling water—heat can set tannin deeper
Chlorine bleach on white cotton only—don't use on colored fabrics
escalation: Red wine on white cotton rarely requires professional help. If stain persists after NSD + vinegar + OxiClean + bleach treatment, it may be a stubborn or very old stain—professional dry cleaning with specialized tannin solvents might help, but home treatment is usually sufficient.

household.name: Mild Liquid Dish Soap (dye-free)
household.note: Use 1/4 tsp in 1 cup water. Household substitute for professional NSD. NSD for initial tannin suspension. 1 tsp per cup cold water.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: Tannin spotter. Dilute 1:1 with cold water. 2-3 minutes. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: OxiClean
household.note: Oxygen bleach. 1 scoop per cup cold water. 15-30 min soak.

professional.name: Tannin Spotter (Professional)
professional.note: Professional-grade tannin remover for set stains.

customerExplanation: Red wine on white cotton is one of the easiest stains because cotton tolerates aggressive treatment. Fresh wine responds to boiling water; older wine needs vinegar, then oxygen bleach. We can even use chlorine bleach on white cotton if needed. Most cases resolve completely.
commonMistakes: Using hot water on aged stains—sets tannin deeper instead of removing it
Skipping NSD and jumping to bleach—NSD removes much of the stain without chemicals
Not doing boiling water pour-through for fresh stains—this is remarkably effective and free
scienceNote: Anthocyanins are polyphenolic pigments that absorb light in the red-blue range. They bond to cellulose through hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces. Heat breaks hydrogen bonds (which is why hot water removes fresh wine), and oxidation breaks the conjugated double-bond system in anthocyanins (which is why bleach and oxygen bleach remove color). This is why we use cold NSD first (for mechanical removal), then heat or oxidation (for chemical removal). White cotton's cellulose structure is unharmed by any of these approaches.
callPro: Rarely needed on white cotton. If stain persists after full protocol, professional dry cleaning with tannin-specific solvents is possible but not usually necessary.
solventNote: Professional: Soak in cold NSD (Mulsolite, 5-10 min). Rinse. Tannin spotter (acetic acid or professional tannin remover, 5-10 min). Rinse. OxiClean or Clorox 2 soak (15-30 min). For white only: dilute chlorine bleach (NaOCl 0.5%, 1-2 min max). Hot water wash with detergent. VDS post-treatment if needed.
